questions.question_text: Colorado's state fair housing law is called the:
questions.explanation: The Colorado Anti-Discrimination Act (CADA) is the state's fair housing law. It is enforced by the Colorado Civil Rights Division (CCRD) within DORA.

questions.question_text: Which agency enforces the Colorado Anti-Discrimination Act?
questions.explanation: CADA is enforced by the Colorado Civil Rights Division (CCRD), which operates within DORA. The Real Estate Commission handles licensing discipline, not fair housing enforcement.

questions.question_text: A landlord who owns a 40-unit apartment building refuses to rent to a tenant solely because the tenant uses Section 8 housing vouchers. Under Colorado law, this is:
distractors: Legal if the landlord prefers cash
Legal if the building has more than four units
questions.explanation: Source of income is protected under CADA (HB20-1332, effective 2021) — landlords cannot refuse tenants solely for using housing vouchers or public assistance. Note the exemptions: the protection doesn't apply to landlords with three or fewer rental units, and owners of five or fewer single-family rental homes aren't required to accept housing choice vouchers.

questions.question_text: Which protected class is recognized by Colorado but was NOT in the original federal Fair Housing Act?
questions.explanation: Colorado protects marital status under CADA; it has never been a federal protected class. Race, national origin, and religion were all in the original 1968 Act (familial status and disability were added in 1988).

questions.question_text: Colorado's fair housing law protects all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: CADA protects sexual orientation, source of income, marital status, and other classes. Political affiliation is not a protected class under Colorado fair housing law.

questions.question_text: Compared to the federal Fair Housing Act, Colorado's 'Mrs. Murphy' exemption is:
questions.explanation: Colorado's version is far narrower: under C.R.S. 24-34-502(8), the owner-occupied exemption (up to four families) applies ONLY to familial status. Unlike the federal Mrs. Murphy exemption, which covers most protected classes, a Colorado owner-occupant gets no exemption for the other protected classes.

questions.question_text: A property owner refuses to rent to an unmarried couple. Under Colorado law, this is:
distractors: Legal under state and federal law
Legal if based on religious beliefs
questions.explanation: Colorado protects marital status under CADA. Refusing to rent to someone because they are unmarried is discrimination, even if it is not prohibited by federal law.

questions.question_text: Sexual orientation as a protected class in housing is explicitly listed under:
questions.correct_answer: Colorado's CADA, but not the federal Fair Housing Act
distractors: The federal Fair Housing Act, but not CADA
Both CADA and the federal Fair Housing Act
questions.explanation: CADA expressly lists sexual orientation, gender identity, and gender expression (C.R.S. 24-34-502). The federal Fair Housing Act does not list sexual orientation as a protected class — federal protection has depended on agency interpretation of 'sex,' which has shifted — so Colorado's explicit statutory protection is broader than federal law.

questions.question_text: Which Colorado fair housing protection has no equivalent in the federal Fair Housing Act?
questions.explanation: Source of income protection (housing vouchers, public assistance) is a Colorado state protection not found in the federal Fair Housing Act. Disability, familial status, and national origin are all federal classes.

questions.question_text: A tenant using public assistance applies for a rental. The landlord asks about income source. Under CADA, asking is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted, but refusing based on it is not
distractors: Permitted and refusal is also permitted
Only permitted for commercial lease applicants
questions.explanation: Landlords can verify income to determine ability to pay rent, but they cannot refuse a tenant solely because their income comes from public assistance or vouchers.

questions.question_text: A housing discrimination complaint under CADA may be filed with the CCRD within:
questions.correct_answer: One year of the alleged violation
distractors: Two years of the alleged violation
questions.explanation: Housing discrimination complaints under CADA must be filed within one year, which aligns with the federal Fair Housing Act deadline for HUD complaints.

questions.question_text: A person files a housing discrimination complaint with the CCRD. The CCRD has a work-sharing agreement with:
questions.explanation: The CCRD has a work-sharing agreement with HUD, meaning complaints can be cross-filed. A state complaint may be investigated at the federal level and vice versa.

questions.question_text: Remedies available under CADA for fair housing violations include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: CADA provides civil remedies: actual damages, injunctive relief, civil penalties, and attorney fees. Criminal imprisonment is not a remedy under CADA.

questions.question_text: After receiving a fair housing complaint, the CCRD's first step is typically to:
distractors: File a lawsuit in district court
Refer the case to federal HUD
questions.explanation: The CCRD investigates complaints and attempts mediation. If mediation fails, the case can be referred for a formal hearing. License revocation is handled by the Real Estate Commission, not CCRD.

questions.question_text: A tenant believes they were discriminated against 14 months ago. Their administrative housing complaint (CCRD or HUD) is:
questions.correct_answer: Time-barred under both state and federal law
distractors: Still timely under state law only
Still timely under federal law only
questions.explanation: Both CADA and the federal Fair Housing Act give one year to file an administrative complaint (CCRD or HUD), so at 14 months an agency complaint is time-barred. A private lawsuit under the federal Act has a separate two-year deadline.
